content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 22, 1997

Russell H. Miller
Attorney at Law
20 Park Road, Suite E
Burlingame, California 94010

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-159

Dear Mr. Miller:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you have not disclosed the identities of the persons
for whom you seek this advice, we are treating this as a request for informal assistance," as
required under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter
should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. Further,
this letter is based on the facts surrounding your questions as you have presented them to us. The
Commission does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71).

QUESTIONS

1. Does Government Code Section 85702 apply to the delivery of noncontrolled
recipient committee (PAC) contributions, Small Contributor Committee (SCC)
contributions, ballot measure committee contributions, political party committee
contributions and/or independent expenditure committee contributions, when the person
delivering the contribution is not the writer of the check or the provider of the services?

2. What meaning do the terms "intermediary" and "conduit" have in Government Code
Section 85702?

___________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear in title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3)).

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-159
Page 2

3. What effect does existing FPPC Regulation 18432.5, defining the term "intermediary"
for purposes of Government Code Section 84302, have on Government Code Section
85702?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Contributions made from any committee, other than the candidate's campaign
committee, will be regulated by Section by 85702 if the contribution is made to or on behalf of a
candidate or to a committee controlled by the candidate. If the person delivering the contribution
is an intermediary or conduit, Section 85702 will require that the contribution be treated as being
made from both the contributor and the intermediary/conduit for purposes of the contribution
limits of Proposition 208.

2. For purposes of Section 85702, a person is an intermediary for a contribution if the
recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the contributor without the
disclosure of the identity of the true source of the contribution. A person is a conduit when he or
she delivers the contribution to the candidate and/or endorses a contribution check over to the
ultimate recipient.

3. The definition of intermediary, for purposes of Section 85702, is the one provided in
Regulation 18432.5.

FACTS

You represent certain individuals who might be considered to be intermediaries or
conduits under Government Code Section 85702. These individuals will be delivering
contributions from some or all of the following committees: noncontrolled recipient committees
(PAC's), Small Contributor Committees, ballot measure committees, political party committees
and independent expenditure committees. The individuals you represent will not be the writers
of the contribution check or the providers of any service. You have not provided us with the
identities of the individuals nor have you given us a specific description of the activities in which
these individuals will be engaged.

ANALYSIS

Subject to specific and limited exceptions, Section 85702 of the California Political
Reform Act of 1996 ("Proposition 208") provides that contributions made to or on behalf of a
candidate through an intermediary or conduit shall be treated as contributions from both the
contributor and the intermediary/conduit for purposes of the contribution limits of Proposition
208. However, this section only applies if the contribution is made to a committee controlled by a
candidate and, therefore, would apply if the contribution is made to a committee controlled by
the candidate. Contributions made to a committee that is not controlled by a candidate are not

File No. A-97-159
Page 3

subject to the restrictions of Section 85702. (Lightman Advice Letter, No. A-96-363).

The term "intermediary" is not defined in Proposition 208, and therefore, the definitions and provisions of the Act will apply to define the term. See Section 85202. We previously have advised that a person is an intermediary for a contribution if the recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true source of the contribution. (Miller Advice Letter, No. I-96-242). This definition of intermediary is contained in Regulation 18432.5 which states, on its face, that the definition is provided for purposes of interpretation of Government Code Section 84302 relating to disclosure requirements governing the making of contributions by an intermediary or agent acting on behalf of another.³ Because the focus of Section 84302 is on mandating disclosure of the identities of intermediaries and agents and, further, is more general in its application, we believe the definition of intermediary as set forth in Regulation 18432.5 was not intended to be used exclusively to interpret Section 84302 but may be applied elsewhere for instance, Regulations 18419 and 18945.3). Accordingly, since Section 85702 employs the term intermediary, but does not define it as it relates to the content of that section, we believe the definition of intermediary, for purposes of Section 85702, is the one provided in Regulation 18432.5 and used in the Miller Advice Letter referenced above.

The term "conduit" also is not defined in Proposition 208 nor is it specifically defined elsewhere in the Act. The distinction between an intermediary and a conduit (thereby giving form to a definition of conduit) was addressed by this office in the Hundt Advice Letter, No. A-76-337 (copy enclosed). We stated in that advice letter,

"[for] example, the PAC is not an agent or intermediary [and is, therefore, a conduit] if an employee makes a check payable to a candidate or candidate's committee and the PAC merely delivers the check to the candidate. Even if the employee makes a check payable to the PAC and the PAC endorses the check over to the ultimate recipient, the PAC has not acted as an agent or intermediary [but, instead, has acted as a conduit]."

We see no reason to depart from the distinctions drawn in the Hundt letter, and thus, we define the term conduit, for purposes of Section 85702, to include a person/entity who delivers a contribution to a candidate and/or who endorses a contribution check over to the recipient.

³ As you accurately point out, prior to the promulgation of Regulation 18432.5, the definition of intermediary (for purposes of Section 84302) had been developed by advice letter, namely the Wilson Advice Letter (No. A-79-077). By way of Proposition 68, Section 84302.5 was enacted in June of 1988 and broadened the definition of intermediary to include (subject to specified exceptions) persons who simply transmitted or delivered contributions. On November 1, 1990, the California Supreme Court invalidated all provisions of Proposition 68 including Section 84302.5. Regulation 18432.5 was then adopted to provide a formal definition of intermediary in the wake of the invalidation of Section 84302.5.

File No. A-97-159
Page 4

As discussed previously, the limitations of Section 85702 apply only to contributions made to or on behalf of a candidate and to contributions made to a committee controlled by a candidate. If the contributions you contemplate fall within these parameters, Section 85702 will govern. Once Section 85702 applies, the distinction between the terms "intermediary" and "conduit" become irrelevant since Section 85702 applies to each type of entity equally: "[c]ontributions made directly or indirectly to or on behalf of a particular candidate through an intermediary or conduit shall be treated as contributions from the contributor and the intermediary or conduit ... for the purposes of this limitation...." (Section 85702.) We have interpreted previously the phrase "contribution from the contributor and the intermediary or conduit" to include both the contributor and the intermediary or conduit. (Moll Advice Letter, No. I-97-013). Therefore, for purposes of Proposition 208's contribution limits, the full amount of any contribution made would be attributable to the contributor and the intermediary or conduit as those terms have been defined above.

Based upon the limited information you have provided, i.e., that the persons you represent will be delivering contributions but not writing checks or providing services, we conclude that these persons are, at least, conduits under Section 85702.

Finally, the existence and nature of the various committees you have referenced in your letter as the source of the contributions do not alter the above analysis. Section 85702 specifies the circumstances under which contributions will be aggregated for limitation purposes; a contribution is a broad term which is defined in Section 82015 of the Act to include "any transfer of anything of value received by a committee from another committee...." Therefore, the fact that a committee is the source of the contribution to be delivered does not result in exclusion from Section 85702. Moreover, Section 85702(a)(1) provides that all "committees," other than the candidate's campaign committee, are included within the ambit of that Section. No other provision of Proposition 208 or the Act exempts specific committees from the Section 85702 requirements.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Lisa L. Ditora
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LD:ak

You represent certain individuals who might be considered to be intermediaries or
conduits under Government Code Section 85702. These individuals will be delivering
contributions from some or all of the following committees: noncontrolled recipient committees
(PAC’s), Small Contributor Committees, ballot measure committees, political party committees

sections.analysis:
Subject to specific and limited exceptions, Section 85702 of the California Political
Reform Act of 1996 (“Proposition 208”) provides that contributions made to or on behalf of a
candidate through an intermediary or conduit shall be treated as contributions from both the
contributor and the intermediary/conduit for purposes of the contribution limits ofProposition
208. However, this section only applies if the contribution is made to or on behalf of a particular
candidate and, therefore, would apply if the contribution is made to a committee controlled by
the candidate. Contributions made to a committee that is not controlled by a candidate are not
subject to the restrictions of Section 85702. (Lightman Advice Letter, No. A-96-363).
The term “intermediary” is not defined in Proposition 208, and therefore, the
definitions and provisions of the Act will apply to define the term. See Section 85202. We
previously have advised that a person is an intermediary for a contribution if the recipient of
the contribution would consider the person to be the contributor without the disclosure of the
identity ofthe true source ofthe contribution. (Miller Advice Letter , No. 1-96-242). This
definition of intermediary is contained in Regulation 18432.5 which states, on its face, that
the definition is provided for purposes of interpretation of Government Code Section 84302
relating to disclosure requirements governing the making of contributions by an intermediary
or agent acting on behalf of another.
3 Because the focus of Section 84302 is on mandating
disclosure of the identities of intermediaries and agents and, further, is more general in its
application, we believe the definition ofintermediary as set forth in Regulation 18432.5 was
not intended to be used exclusively to interpret Section 84302 but may be applied elsewhere
in the Act when the term intermediary is not given a specific and distinct meaning (compare,
for instance, Regulations 18419 and 18945.3). Accordingly, since Section 85702 employs
the term intermediary, but does not define it as it relates to the content of that section, we
believe the definition of intermediary, for purposes of Section 85702, is the one provided in
Regulation 18432.5 and used in the Miller Advice Letter referenced above.
The term “conduit” also is not defined in Proposition 208 nor is it specifically defined
elsewhere in the Act. The distinction between an intermediary and a conduit (thereby giving
form to a definition of conduit) was addressed by this office in the Hundt Advice Letter, No.
A-76-337 (copy enclosed). We stated in that advice letter,
“[for] example, the PAC is not an agent or intermediary [and is, therefore, a
conduit] if an employee makes a check payable to a candidate or candidate’s
committee and the PAC merely delivers the check to the candidate. Even if
the employee makes a check payable to the PAC and the PAC endorses the
check over to the ultimate recipient, the PAC has not acted as an agent or
intermediary I:but, instead, has acted as a conduit].”
We see no reason to depart from the distinctions drawn in the Hundt letter, and thus, we
define the term conduit, for purposes of Section 85702, to include a person/entity who
delivers a contribution to a candidate and/or who endorses a contribution check over to the
recipient.
As you accurately point out, prior to the promulgation of Regulation 18432.5, the definition of
intennediary (for purposes of Section 84302) had been developed by advice letter, namely the Wilson Advice Letter
(No. A-79-077). By way of Proposition 68, Section 84302.5 was enacted in June of 1988 and broadened the
definition of intermediary to include (subject to specified exceptions) persons who simply transmitted or delivered
contributions. On November 1, 1990, the California Supreme Court invalidated all provisions of Proposition 68
including Section 84302.5. Regulation 18432.5 was then adopted to provide a formal definition of intermediary in
the wake of the invalidation of Section 84302.5.
As discussed previously, the limitations of Section 85702 apply only to contributions
made to or on behalf of a candidate and to contributions made to a committee controlled by a
candidate. If the contributions you contemplate fall within these parameters, Section 85702
will govern. Once Section 85702 applies, the distinction between the terms “intermediary”
and “conduit” become irrelevant since Section 85702 applies to each type of entity equally:
“[cjontributions made directly or indirectly to or on behalfofa particular candidate through
an intermediary or conduit shall be treated as contributions from the contributor and the
intermediary or conduit ... for the purposes of this limitation....” (Section 85702.) We have
interpreted previously the phrase “contribution from the contributor and the intermediary or
conduit” to include both the contributor and the intermediary or conduit. (Molt Advice
Letter, No. 1-97-0 13). Therefore, for purposes ofProposition 208’s contribution limits, the
full amount of any contribution made would be attributable to the contributor and the
intermediary or conduit as those terms have been defined above.
Based upon the limited information you have provided, i.e., that the persons you
represent will be delivering contributions but not writing checks or providing services, we
conclude that these persons are, at least, conduits under Section 85702.
Finally, the existence and the nature of the various committees you have referenced in
your letter as the source ofthe contributions do not alter the above analysis. Section 85702
specifies the circumstances under which contributions will be aggregated for limitation
purposes; a contribution is a broad term which is defined in Section 82015 of the Act to
include “any transfer of anything of value received by a committee from another
committee....” Therefore, the fact that a committee is the source of the contribution to be
delivered does not result in exclusion from Section 85702. Moreover, Section 85702(a)(1)
provides that all “committees,” other than the candidate’s campaign committee, are included
within the ambit of that Section. No other provision ofProposition 208 or the Act exempts
specific committees from the Section 85702 requirements.
